species: Rocky Mountain Elk -- herd full bugle
sound_type: Full herd bugling event: multiple bulls (2-6+) bugling simultaneously in rutting season; each bull produces the complete bugle sequence (low-frequency bellow rise, 400-600 Hz high harmonic peak, descending grunt sequence); overlapping temporal structure when multiple bulls respond; acoustic landscape filled with bugling across the full herd territory for periods of 5-60 minutes during peak rut dawn and dusk activity windows
ecosystem: Subalpine meadow-forest interface -- open meadow and parkland surrounded by old-growth or mature coniferous forest; the meadow provides the acoustic open space for bugling to carry maximum distance; the forest provides thermal cover and calving/bedding habitat; the interface is where elk rut activity and thus full-herd bugling peaks
season: Rutting season: mid-September through mid-October (peak: late September). Full-herd bugling events are concentrated at dawn and dusk during peak rut. Individual bull bugling extends before and after peak season for territorial maintenance.
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Rocky Mountain Elk populations healthy in Cascade Range, Olympic Peninsula, and eastern Washington. Active management under Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife. Recovering populations in areas of predator reintroduction (including Wolf recovery, SPS 313 cross-reference).
milestone: FIRST ELK HERD ENTRY IN ENGINE. E=8. First full-herd bugling acoustic event documented. The most acoustically complex single-species PNW mammal event in the engine: multiple simultaneous bull bugles overlapping in frequency and time. POST-SUMMIT ENERGY DESCENT BEGINS: E=8 vs. E=9 in degree 313. The engine begins the post-Summit gradient.

coordinates.description: Cascade Range east slope and subalpine zones: primary full-herd bugling habitat. Mount Rainier National Park (elevation 1,500-2,000m), Olympic National Park (subalpine meadows), Wenatchee National Forest (Naches River corridor), Yakama Nation (Simcoe Mountains). Rocky Mountain Elk primary subspecies in Washington State. Multiple large herds documented.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Elk bugle: three-phase sequence. Phase 1: low-frequency bellow-rise (100-300 Hz, 1-2 seconds). Phase 2: high harmonic peak (400-600 Hz primary, harmonics to 2,000 Hz, 2-4 seconds -- the characteristic high whistle). Phase 3: descending grunt sequence (80-200 Hz, multiple grunts, 1-3 seconds). Full-herd event: multiple bulls in overlapping bugle sequences, temporal offset of 5-30 seconds between bulls, acoustic landscape filled for extended periods.
acoustic_data.mechanics: Elk bugle produced by laryngeal vocalization with resonance in extended nasal passage. The high-frequency peak (400-600 Hz primary) is unusual for a 300-450 kg animal -- most large ungulates produce primary energy at 100-300 Hz. The elevated fundamental frequency is the elk's acoustic signature. Multi-bull overlap: bulls respond to each other's bugles; each new bugle triggers responses from nearby bulls; the acoustic event compounds over time.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: Individual bugle: 80-2,000 Hz (full three-phase sequence). Full-herd event: same range but with temporal overlap producing a near-continuous acoustic field at 80-2,000 Hz for the duration of the peak activity window.
acoustic_data.duration: Individual bugle: 5-10 seconds (full three phases). Peak activity window: 30-60 minutes at dawn and dusk during peak rut. During this window, the acoustic landscape is continuously occupied by overlapping bugles.
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8: Four factors: (1) acoustic volume: bull elk bugle reaches 90-100 dB at 100m -- audible at 1-2 km in open terrain; (2) multi-bull overlap: full-herd event is acoustically denser than any single bull, covering the landscape in overlapping acoustic events; (3) frequency signature: the elevated primary frequency (400-600 Hz 'whistle') against the low grunt sequence is acoustically distinctive and carries emotional weight beyond pure volume; (4) ecological context: full-herd rut is the Pacific Northwest's largest terrestrial mammal acoustic event by species population. Full-herd bugling: E=8. Individual bull bugling: E=7. The herd context is the single energy-increment factor.

nodes.title: First Elk Herd Entry -- Most Complex Single-Species PNW Mammal Acoustic Event
nodes.description: The elk herd full bugle is the most acoustically complex single-species mammal event in the PNW engine: multiple bulls in overlapping temporal structure, same frequency range, responding to each other, filling the landscape for extended periods. No other PNW mammal produces this kind of collective single-species acoustic density.

nodes.title: Post-Summit Energy Gradient Begins -- E=8 at Degree 314
nodes.description: Degrees 311-313 were all E=9 on the SPS track. Degree 314 is E=8. The post-Summit energy descent begins here. The engine is moving from the maximum zone through the high-energy zone. E=8 is not diminishment -- it is the second-highest calibration in the engine -- but the gradient has begun.

nodes.title: Wolf-Elk Trophic Cascade (Cross-Reference SPS 313)
nodes.description: Gray Wolf recolonization (SPS 313) directly affects Elk herd behavior. In areas with Wolf presence, Elk herds avoid overgrazing riparian corridors -- the 'ecology of fear' effect documented in Yellowstone. Wolf recolonization in northeast Washington (SPS 313) means the acoustic ecology of degree 314 (Elk bugling) is altered by the biological event documented in the previous degree. The engine's consecutive entries are ecologically connected.

nodes.title: Acoustic Landscape Filling -- E=8 Through Collective Not Individual
nodes.description: The Elk herd bugling event is E=8 through collective density rather than individual extremity. Single bull = E=7. Multiple bulls in overlap = E=8. The increment comes from the landscape-filling quality: no single individual reaches E=8, but the herd collectively occupies every corner of the acoustic landscape during peak activity. Collective E=8 is a different calibration mechanism than individual E=8.

nodes.title: Pearl Jam -- Elk Herd Equivalence
nodes.description: Pearl Jam's sonic signature: full-band collective commitment, McCready melodic lead across the full register, Vedder's baritone filling the acoustic space below the band. The band fills the acoustic landscape collectively; no single Pearl Jam element is the maximum, but the full band together produces E=8 consistently. Elk herd bugling: no single bull is E=8, but the herd collectively fills the landscape. Same calibration mechanism: collective landscape-filling = E=8.

cross_references.relationship: E=8 + E=8 = E=16. First degree below E=18 in post-Summit sequence. Collective-landscape-filling equivalence: Pearl Jam fills the acoustic space through full-band commitment; Elk herd fills through multi-bull overlap. Both E=8 through collective density.

Wolf-Elk trophic cascade: Wolf presence (313) alters Elk herd behavior (314). The ecology of fear. Consecutive engine degrees document ecologically connected events. Wolf recolonization (313) directly affects the acoustic behavior of Elk herds (314).
Previous E=8 terrestrial mammal entry. Grizzly: individual maximum, E=8. Elk herd: collective maximum, E=8. Two different mechanisms for reaching E=8 on the terrestrial mammal track.
Individual bull bugling is E=7. Full-herd is E=8. The engine will likely document individual bull entry in a future degree at E=7, distinguishing individual from collective calibration.
